Psalm 135 opens with an urgent summons: "Praise the name of the LORD." Not praise in general, but praise of His name—His character, His identity, His revealed nature. The psalmist calls specifically to those who stand in the house of God, those positioned to witness His glory most directly. Yet this is not an exclusive command. It ripples outward to all Israel, to all who fear the LORD. What strikes us immediately is the reason for this praise: the LORD is good, and His goodness is not abstract or distant. It is demonstrated in concrete history. He chose Jacob and Israel as His treasured possession. He is not a distant deity indifferent to His people; He has chosen them, claimed them, made them His own. This is the foundation of worship—not duty, but gratitude for electing love.

The psalm then moves to the power that backs this choosing. The LORD is great and above all gods because He does whatever pleases Him. This is not mere poetic exaggeration. Verses 6 through 12 catalog real events: God controls weather and creation, He devastated Egypt with the plagues, He destroyed the kings blocking Israel's path to Canaan. This is not mythological decoration. The psalmist is saying that the same God who dwells with His people and knows them by name is the God who commands the forces of nature and history. His renown endures forever because His deeds endure forever. What He has done cannot be undone or forgotten.

Against this backdrop of divine power and faithfulness comes a stark contrast: the idols of the nations. These are not subtle threats or clever philosophical alternatives. They are dead things—silver and gold, shaped by human hands, possessing mouths that cannot speak, eyes that cannot see, ears that cannot hear. The psalmist lingers on their complete and utter impotence, and then delivers a sobering truth: "Those who make them become like them, as do all who trust in them." To trust in what is dead is to become dead. To worship what cannot see, hear, or act is to lose your own perception, hearing, and agency. This is not mere mockery. This is a warning about the spiritual reality of idolatry—it deadens the soul.

The psalm concludes by calling together all of Israel—the house of Israel, the house of Aaron, the house of Levi, all who fear the LORD—to join in blessing God. The blessing descends from Zion, from the place where God's name dwells. The repeated call to bless the LORD is a summons to align yourself with reality as it truly is. God is sovereign, faithful, active, and deserving of all praise. To bless Him is to acknowledge what is true. To withhold praise is to live in denial of the God who has already chosen you, who will vindicate you, who shows compassion to His servants. The question the psalm leaves with you is not theoretical: Will you be among those who praise the living God, or will you become like the silent idols you trust?
